channel.title: Questions on Wang's Harmony Acupuncture
channel.description: Recent content in Questions on Wang's Harmony Acupuncture

item.title: What Is Acupuncture?
item.description:
Acupuncture is a core part of Traditional Chinese Medicine and has been used for centuries to help the body regulate pain, stress, and other symptoms.

In Chinese medicine, health is connected to balance in yin and yang, qi (chee), blood, and the functional harmony of organs and meridians. When that balance shifts, symptoms can develop.

Acupuncture uses very thin needles placed at specific points on the body to help restore balance. The goal is to support the body’s natural healing response and ease symptoms over time.

item.title: Does Acupuncture Hurt?
item.description:
Acupuncture needles are hair-thin and very different from the hollow needles used for injections or blood draws. For most people, insertion feels minimal or only briefly noticeable.

Points on the hands, feet, and face can feel a bit more sensitive, but that usually passes quickly.

During treatment, some people notice soreness, heaviness, warmth, coolness, or brief tingling. These sensations are usually temporary.

After treatment, many people feel calm and deeply relaxed. That sense of settling is often one of the first things they notice.

item.title: How Much Does Mobile Acupuncture Cost?
item.description:
Our mobile service has a base commute rate of $29.99 per commute.

Before booking further appointments, new patients complete a brief virtual consultation for $20.

We keep rates clear and offer package options and senior discounts so ongoing care can feel more practical.

The best next step is usually the brief consultation, because it lets you discuss symptoms, goals, and how often treatment may make sense before you commit to a plan.

item.title: How Many Sessions Will I Need?
item.description:
The number of sessions depends on your condition and how long it has been going on.

In general, long-standing issues take longer to treat than newer concerns.

Acupuncture works best as a process, so it can take time to see clear and lasting results.

A common starting point is twice per week for severe pain, or weekly treatment for four to six weeks for many other concerns before judging progress.

item.title: What Can Acupuncture Treat?
item.description:
Some of the most common reasons patients come to us include:

  • Pain and injury recovery, including neck pain, back pain, headaches, and mobility concerns
  • Women’s health and fertility support, including cycle health, IVF support, postpartum recovery, and menopause
  • Stress, sleep, and wellness support, including insomnia, anxiety, fatigue, and burnout
  • Digestive and metabolic support, including IBS, bloating, reflux, and appetite changes
  • Respiratory, immune, and skin support, including sinus symptoms, allergies, cough, and repeat flare-ups

Acupuncture can be a useful part of a broader care plan, but it does not replace needed medical evaluation. For example, after an injury it is smart to see a medical doctor first to rule out fractures or structural damage before starting treatment.

item.title: What Are the Risks of Acupuncture?
item.description:
Licensed acupuncturists are trained in safe needling, so the risk of serious side effects is very low.

The most common side effects are minor bleeding, mild bruising, or occasional dizziness after treatment.

Special caution is used for people taking blood thinner medication.

Electro-acupuncture may not be suitable for people with pacemakers. Non-electric treatment is still available.

Before treatment, it helps to mention any blood thinner use, pacemaker, pregnancy, history of fainting, or major medical condition so care can be adjusted appropriately.

item.title: What Is Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM)?
item.description:
Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) is a long-standing system of care that looks at patterns in the body rather than only isolated symptoms.

Instead of focusing on just one complaint, TCM looks at how symptoms connect with sleep, stress, digestion, energy, hormones, and recovery patterns.

Its framework includes ideas like yin and yang, the Five Elements, organ systems, meridians, and syndrome differentiation. In practice, treatment is based on the whole picture rather than one symptom alone.
